text:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, enabling the rapid quantification of cellular metabolites based on their mass-to-charge ratios and chromatographic retention times. With the advent of high-resolution, fast-scanning mass spectrometers, there has been a growing trend toward high-throughput screening using minimal or even no chromatographic separation. However, when analyzing complex biological extracts, the absence of prior separation can lead to the serious misannotation of metabolites. 

In this study, we demonstrate that even electrospray ionization—widely considered a "soft" ionization technique—produces significant in-source fragmentation. These unintended byproducts often possess the same exact mass as other common metabolites. For instance, we show that nucleotide-triphosphates frequently fragment into nucleotide-diphosphates, while hexose-phosphates can degrade into triose-phosphates within the ion source. By evaluating yeast intracellular extracts, we identified over 20 instances where in-source fragments effectively "mimic" endogenous metabolites, potentially leading to substantial errors in quantification. Our findings underscore that robust chromatographic separation remains essential for the accurate identification and measurement of many central cellular metabolites.
